skill.name: ubiquitous-language
skill.description: Extract a DDD-style ubiquitous language glossary from the current conversation, flagging ambiguities and proposing canonical terms. Saves to UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md. Use when user wants to define domain terms, build a glossary, harden terminology, create a ubiquitous language, or mentions "domain model" or "DDD".
skill.summary: Builds a Domain-Driven Design glossary from conversation context, identifying ambiguous terms and proposing canonical definitions.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: This skill is a benign Domain-Driven Design (DDD) documentation tool. All static findings were evaluated and dismissed as false positives: the 'backtick execution' patterns are markdown code blocks containing template text, the 'cryptographic algorithm' match is the word 'algorithm' in a DDD context description, and 'system reconnaissance' matches are markdown table rows with domain term definitions. No malicious intent or dangerous code patterns were found.

content.user_title: Extract DDD Ubiquitous Language Glossaries From Conversations
content.value_statement: Domain discussions often mix synonyms and overloaded terms that cause confusion. This skill scans conversations to identify domain terms, flag ambiguities, and write a canonical glossary to UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md.
actual_capabilities: Scans conversations for domain-relevant nouns, verbs, and concepts
Identifies synonyms, ambiguities, and overloaded terms
Proposes canonical term choices with opinionated recommendations
Writes a structured UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md file with grouped term tables
Generates example dialogues demonstrating proper term usage
Flags ambiguities with clear resolution recommendations
limitations: Requires `disable-model-invocation: true` and must be manually triggered by the user
Only captures terms discussed in the current conversation context
Does not analyze code repositories for domain terms automatically
Cannot enforce glossary usage across a team's codebase

use_cases.title: Onboarding New Developers
use_cases.description: Extract a domain glossary from architecture discussions so new team members can learn the canonical terms used in the project.

use_cases.title: Refining Domain Models
use_cases.description: Review conversations about business requirements to identify terms that need disambiguation before implementing a domain model.

use_cases.title: Aligning With Domain Experts
use_cases.description: Produce a shared glossary that both developers and domain experts can reference during requirement-gathering sessions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Glossary Extraction
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ubiquitous-language skill to extract a glossary from our current conversation about the order management system.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have just finished a domain discussion and want to capture the terms

prompt_templates.title: Resolving Term Conflicts
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the ubiquitous-language skill and pay special attention to the conflicting uses of 'account' in our discussion about customer management.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you suspect certain words are being used ambiguously

prompt_templates.title: Building a Subdomain Glossary
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the ubiquitous-language skill to our conversation about billing and invoicing. Group terms by subdomain (billing, payments, invoicing) in separate tables.
prompt_templates.scenario: When the domain has clear subdomains that benefit from separate term groupings

prompt_templates.title: Updating an Existing Glossary
prompt_templates.prompt: Re-run the ubiquitous-language skill to update UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md with the new terms we just discussed about fulfillment and shipping.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have an existing glossary and need to incorporate new discussion

output_examples.input: Discussion about order management where 'account' was used for both customers and authentication users.
output: A UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md file with tables grouped by domain area (Order lifecycle, People, Relationships)
An 'Order' term defined as 'A customer request to purchase one or more items' with aliases 'Purchase, transaction'
A flagged ambiguity section noting 'account' was used for both Customer and User concepts

output_examples.input: Brainstorming session about a shipping and fulfillment system.
output: Grouped tables for Fulfillment, Shipment, and Delivery concepts
An example dialogue between a developer and domain expert clarifying how Invoices relate to Fulfillments
A summary message listing the key terms added and ambiguities resolved

best_practices: Run the skill after substantive domain discussions rather than during active brainstorming to get clearer terms.
Review the flagged ambiguities section carefully and resolve conflicts with the domain expert before finalizing the glossary.
Re-run the skill incrementally as the domain understanding evolves rather than starting from scratch each time.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to extract generic programming terms like 'array' or 'function' that have no domain-specific meaning.
Do not force artificial groupings when all terms belong to a single cohesive domain — one table is acceptable.
Do not skip the example dialogue section, as it demonstrates how terms interact in real conversations.

faq.question: What is a ubiquitous language?
faq.answer: A ubiquitous language is a Domain-Driven Design concept where developers and domain experts share a common vocabulary for the business domain, reducing miscommunication.

faq.question: Where does the glossary file get saved?
faq.answer: The glossary is saved to UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md in the current working directory.

faq.question: Can the skill run automatically?
faq.answer: No, the skill uses disable-model-invocation: true and must be manually triggered by the user.

faq.question: How does the skill handle re-runs?
faq.answer: On re-run, the skill reads the existing UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md, incorporates new terms, updates definitions, re-flags ambiguities, and rewrites the example dialogue.

faq.question: What makes a term worth including?
faq.answer: Only include terms that are relevant for domain experts. Skip module names, class names, and generic programming concepts unless they carry domain-specific meaning.

faq.question: Does the skill work with all AI coding assistants?
faq.answer: Yes, it is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as listed in the supported tools.
